name: Cloud Service Models: IaaS, PaaS, SaaS
description: Three fundamental cloud delivery models that define the level of management responsibility shared between the provider and customer.
key_facts: IaaS provides virtualized compute, storage, and networking; customer manages OS, middleware, and apps
PaaS provides a platform for developing and deploying applications; customer manages only the application and data
SaaS delivers fully managed software over the internet; customer only manages user access and data
The shared responsibility model shifts as you move from IaaS to SaaS—provider takes on more responsibility
Azure examples: IaaS=Azure VMs, PaaS=Azure App Service, SaaS=Microsoft 365
common_misconceptions: Many assume PaaS means no infrastructure concerns, but networking and scaling limits still apply
SaaS customers often believe they own the underlying data infrastructure, but only the data itself

name: Shared Responsibility Model
description: A framework defining which security and operational tasks are handled by Microsoft Azure versus the customer.
key_facts: Microsoft is always responsible for the physical datacenter, network, and host hardware
Customer is always responsible for their data, accounts, and access management
IaaS: customer owns OS patches, SaaS: Microsoft owns nearly everything operational
The model varies by deployment type: on-premises, IaaS, PaaS, SaaS
Understanding this model is critical for compliance and security posture planning
common_misconceptions: Customers in SaaS often forget they are still responsible for their own data governance
People assume the cloud provider handles all security, ignoring identity and access responsibilities

name: Cloud Deployment Models: Public, Private, Hybrid
description: The three deployment types that define where cloud infrastructure is hosted and who has access to it.
key_facts: Public cloud: infrastructure owned and operated by a third-party provider like Microsoft Azure
Private cloud: infrastructure dedicated to a single organization, on-premises or hosted
Hybrid cloud: combines public and private clouds, allowing data and apps to move between them
Azure Stack enables organizations to run Azure services in a private/hybrid model
Multi-cloud uses services from two or more public cloud providers
common_misconceptions: Private cloud does not mean on-premises only; it can be hosted by a third party exclusively for one customer
Hybrid cloud does not require equal workload distribution between public and private

name: Azure Regions and Availability Zones
description: The global geographic infrastructure of Azure that enables high availability, disaster recovery, and data residency.
key_facts: Azure has 60+ regions globally, each a set of datacenters connected by low-latency networks
Availability Zones are physically separate datacenters within a region, each with independent power, cooling, and networking
Each region with Availability Zones has at least 3 separate zones
Region Pairs are two Azure regions within the same geography used for geo-redundant backup
Some services are zone-redundant by default; others require explicit zone selection
common_misconceptions: An Availability Zone is not the same as a region; it is a datacenter within a region
Not all Azure regions support Availability Zones; this feature must be verified for specific regions

name: Azure Resource Groups and Azure Resource Manager
description: Logical containers for grouping related Azure resources and the management layer that enables CRUD operations on those resources.
key_facts: A resource group is a container that holds related resources for an Azure solution
All resources must belong to exactly one resource group
Azure Resource Manager (ARM) is the deployment and management service that processes all Azure API requests
ARM templates enable Infrastructure-as-Code deployments using JSON
Access control, policies, and tags applied to a resource group are inherited by all resources in it
common_misconceptions: Resources in the same resource group do not need to be in the same region
Deleting a resource group deletes all resources within it, which is often unexpected

name: Azure Compute Services Overview
description: The range of Azure services for running applications, including virtual machines, containers, and serverless options.
key_facts: Azure Virtual Machines provide IaaS compute with full OS control
Azure App Service is a PaaS offering for hosting web apps, REST APIs, and mobile backends
Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) manages containerized workloads using Kubernetes
Azure Functions is serverless compute that runs code in response to events with pay-per-execution pricing
Azure Container Instances (ACI) offers the fastest and simplest way to run containers without orchestration
common_misconceptions: Azure Functions are not limited to small tasks; they can run long processes with the Durable Functions extension
App Service is not just for web apps; it also hosts APIs, mobile backends, and WebJobs

name: Azure Networking Fundamentals
description: Core networking services in Azure that enable connectivity between resources and to the internet.
key_facts: Azure Virtual Network (VNet) is the fundamental building block for private networks in Azure
VNet peering connects two VNets so resources in each can communicate
Azure VPN Gateway enables encrypted connections between Azure and on-premises networks
Azure ExpressRoute provides dedicated private connectivity from on-premises to Azure without the public internet
Azure DNS hosts DNS domains and provides name resolution using Azure infrastructure
common_misconceptions: VNet peering is not transitive by default; if VNet A peers to B and B peers to C, A cannot reach C without explicit peering
ExpressRoute does not replace VPN; they serve different use cases with ExpressRoute offering higher reliability

name: Azure Storage Services
description: Managed cloud storage services in Azure including blob, file, queue, and table storage.
key_facts: Azure Blob Storage stores unstructured data like images, documents, and backups in tiers: Hot, Cool, Cold, Archive
Azure Files provides fully managed file shares accessible via SMB and NFS protocols
Azure Queue Storage enables asynchronous messaging between application components
Azure Table Storage is a NoSQL key-attribute store for structured non-relational data
Azure Storage accounts are the root container for all Azure Storage services and require a globally unique name
common_misconceptions: Archive tier blob access requires rehydration, which can take hours; it is not instantly accessible
Azure Files is not the same as Blob Storage; it provides a shareable filesystem, not object storage

name: Azure Database Services
description: Managed database offerings in Azure covering relational, NoSQL, and in-memory data platforms.
key_facts: Azure SQL Database is a fully managed relational database based on the latest stable SQL Server engine
Azure Cosmos DB is a globally distributed NoSQL database supporting multiple APIs including MongoDB and Cassandra
Azure Database for MySQL, PostgreSQL, and MariaDB offer managed open-source relational databases
Azure Cache for Redis provides an in-memory data structure store for caching and session management
Azure SQL Managed Instance provides near 100% compatibility with SQL Server for lift-and-shift migrations
common_misconceptions: Azure SQL Database is not the same as SQL Server on an Azure VM; it is a fully managed PaaS service
Cosmos DB is not just a document store; it supports multiple data models via different APIs

name: Azure Identity Services and Microsoft Entra ID
description: Microsoft's cloud-based identity and access management service that enables secure sign-in and access to resources.
key_facts: Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure Active Directory) is the cloud-based identity provider for Azure
Entra ID supports Single Sign-On (SSO), Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA), and Conditional Access
Azure AD Connect synchronizes on-premises Active Directory to Entra ID for hybrid identity
Entra ID Tenants are dedicated instances of Entra ID that an organization receives when signing up
Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) uses Entra ID identities to control access to Azure resources
common_misconceptions: Entra ID is not Windows Active Directory in the cloud; it uses different protocols (OAuth, SAML, OpenID Connect)
MFA in Entra ID does not require on-premises infrastructure; it is fully cloud-managed

name: Azure Policy and Governance
description: Services and tools that help organizations enforce compliance, control costs, and manage resources at scale.
key_facts: Azure Policy evaluates resources against business rules expressed as policy definitions
Management Groups provide a hierarchical governance scope above subscriptions
Azure Blueprints (deprecated in favor of Deployment Stacks) define repeatable sets of Azure resources that implement standards
Azure Cost Management helps monitor, allocate, and optimize cloud spending
Resource tags are name-value pairs attached to resources for organization and cost allocation
common_misconceptions: Azure Policy can prevent non-compliant deployments (Deny effect), not just audit them
Management Groups are optional but essential for large enterprise multi-subscription governance

name: Azure Monitoring and Management Tools
description: Tools for monitoring resource health, performance, and operational insights across Azure environments.
key_facts: Azure Monitor collects metrics and logs from Azure resources and supports alerting and dashboards
Log Analytics Workspace stores and queries log data collected by Azure Monitor
Application Insights is an application performance management (APM) service for developers
Azure Service Health provides status of Azure services and personalized health alerts
Azure Advisor provides personalized best-practice recommendations for reliability, security, performance, and cost
common_misconceptions: Azure Monitor and Log Analytics are separate; Monitor collects data and Log Analytics is where it is stored and queried
Azure Advisor recommendations are proactive and free, but implementing them may have associated service costs

name: Azure Security Tools and Services
description: Security services in Azure that protect workloads against threats, vulnerabilities, and attacks.
key_facts: Microsoft Defender for Cloud provides unified security management and threat protection across Azure and on-premises
Azure DDoS Protection defends Azure resources against distributed denial-of-service attacks
Azure Firewall is a managed, cloud-based network security service that controls traffic
Azure Key Vault securely stores secrets, keys, and certificates with hardware security module backing
Microsoft Sentinel is a cloud-native SIEM/SOAR solution for threat detection and response
common_misconceptions: Defender for Cloud is not just for Azure; it can also protect on-premises and multi-cloud resources
Azure Firewall operates at Layer 4-7; it is not just a simple packet filter

name: Azure Pricing and Total Cost of Ownership
description: Concepts and tools for understanding, estimating, and optimizing the cost of running workloads on Azure.
key_facts: Azure Pricing Calculator estimates the monthly cost of Azure resources before deployment
Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) Calculator compares on-premises costs to Azure over time
Azure Reservations provide up to 72% savings by committing to one or three-year terms
Azure Spot VMs use unused Azure capacity at up to 90% discount, but can be evicted
Azure Hybrid Benefit allows using existing on-premises Windows Server and SQL licenses on Azure
common_misconceptions: The Pricing Calculator is not the same as the TCO Calculator; Pricing estimates Azure costs, TCO compares migration savings
Azure Reservations require upfront payment only in the upfront payment option; monthly billing is also available

name: Azure SLAs and Service Lifecycle
description: Service Level Agreements that define Microsoft's uptime commitments and the lifecycle phases of Azure services.
key_facts: SLAs define the minimum level of service Microsoft guarantees, expressed as monthly uptime percentage
Most Azure services offer 99.9% or higher SLA; 99.99% is common for redundant configurations
Services in preview (Public Preview or Private Preview) have no SLA
The composite SLA of a multi-service application is the product of each service's individual SLA
Azure services progress through Private Preview, Public Preview, and General Availability (GA)
common_misconceptions: Adding availability zones increases SLA from 99.9% to 99.99% for supported services, not to 100%
Preview services are functional and free in some cases, but should not be used for production workloads
